summaries.summary: The enriched WikiPathways pathways predominantly highlight cellular processes related to apoptosis, immune responses, and cellular stress, with a notable presence of pathways involved in viral infections and DNA damage.

The enriched KEGG pathways highlight significant alterations in immune responses, cell signaling, and apoptotic processes in the context of Peripheral Arterial Disease (PAD). These pathways are crucial for understanding the inflammatory and cellular damage associated with PAD.

The enriched Reactome pathways highlight significant alterations in immune responses, cell signaling, and apoptotic processes in the context of Peripheral Arterial Disease (PAD). These pathways are crucial for understanding the inflammatory and cellular stress responses associated with PAD.

The enriched GO terms highlight significant alterations in immune system function and cellular processes in patients with Peripheral Arterial Disease (PAD) compared to controls. Key themes include immune cell activation and cytokine production, apoptotic processes, cellular responses to stress, and vascular-related functions like smooth muscle cell proliferation and angiogenesis. This suggests a complex interplay of inflammation, cell death, and vascular remodeling in PAD.

The ChEA analysis identified several transcription factors (TFs) significantly enriched in the differentially expressed genes (DEGs) from peripheral arterial disease (PAD) patients compared to controls. Key TFs include RELB, RELA, JUND, and RUNX2, all associated with inflammatory and cellular processes. Their enrichment suggests a regulatory network driving the observed gene expression changes in PAD.

The STRING analysis highlights a highly interconnected network of proteins, with FOS, TLR4, and CDKN1A exhibiting the highest network properties. These central nodes interact with a diverse range of proteins involved in inflammatory responses, cell cycle regulation, and immune signaling. The experimental context of Peripheral Arterial Disease (PAD) suggests these interactions may contribute to vascular inflammation and endothelial dysfunction.
